Assess the potential adverse effects associated with metoprolol. Implement interprofessional collaboration when prescribing metoprolol, considering co-administered medications, potential drug interactions, and patient-specific factors while prioritizing open communication and shared decision-making. agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
agent1_snippets.content: Metabolism: Metoprolol is extensively metabolized in the liver by the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2D6. [24] Then it undergoes oxidative deamination and O-demethylation to form several inactive metabolites, such as alpha-hydroxy-metoprolol and 4’-hydroxy-metoprolol. Metoprolol is a racemic mixture of R- and S-enantiomers with different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. The R-enantiomer is more potent and selective for β-1 receptors than the S-enantiomer; it is also more prone to metabolism by CYP2D6. The S-enantiomer has a longer half-life and higher plasma concentration than the R-enantiomer. The metabolism of metoprolol is influenced by genetic polymorphism of CYP2D6, which can result in poor, intermediate, extensive, or ultra-rapid metabolizers. Poor metabolizers have higher plasma levels and longer half-lives of metoprolol than extensive ones, while ultra-rapid metabolizers have lower plasma levels and shorter half-lives than extensive ones. [23]

agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
agent1_snippets.content: Hepatic impairment: Metoprolol is extensively metabolized in the liver by the enzyme CYP2D6. Patients with hepatic impairment may have reduced clearance and increased plasma levels of metoprolol, which may increase the risk of adverse effects. The elimination half-life of metoprolol may be prolonged depending on the severity of hepatic impairment. Therefore, metoprolol should be used with caution and at lower doses in patients with hepatic impairment. [27]

agent1_snippets.title: Mirabegron -- Monitoring
agent1_snippets.content: The liver’s CYP450 system predominantly metabolizes mirabegron. Monitoring and dose adjustment should occur with patients on other medications with a narrow therapeutic index and also undergo metabolism via CYP2D6. [13] According to American Family Physicians (AAFP), potent inhibitors of the CYP2D6 system are amiodarone, diphenhydramine, fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine, ritonavir, and terbinafine. Substrates of the CYP2D6 system are amitriptyline, carvedilol, codeine, donepezil, haloperidol, metoprolol, paroxetine, risperidone, and tramadol. There are no significant inducers. In this light, it is vital to consider all comorbidities and drugs patients are on to prevent undesirable drug-drug interactions. It is recommended to take a thorough history, considering past medical history and review of medications before starting mirabegron therapy. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
agent1_snippets.content: Metoprolol undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism, primarily mediated by CYP2D6. Co-administration with substances that modulate CYP2D6 activity can lead to altered Metoprolol concentrations; these include: Inhibitors (eg, fluoxetine, paroxetine) may elevate metoprolol levels, potentially leading to enhanced β-blockade and adverse effects. CYP2D6 inducers: Conversely, CYP2D6 inducers (eg, rifampin) may accelerate metoprolol metabolism, potentially reducing its therapeutic efficacy. Dose adjustments or alternative therapeutic options may be warranted. Calcium channel blockers: Concurrent use of calcium channel blockers, particularly verapamil and diltiazem, may lead to additive negative inotropic and chronotropic effects, potentially exacerbating bradycardia and hypotension. Drug- Drug Interaction and Adverse Events

agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Metoprolol, a widely employed β-adrenergic antagonist, is pivotal in cardiovascular pharmacology. Metoprolol is indicated primarily for conditions such as hypertension, angina pectoris, heart failure, and arrhythmias. The medication exerts its therapeutic effect by selectively inhibiting β-1 adrenergic receptors, resulting in diminished cardiac contractility, heart rate, and blood pressure. While generally well-tolerated, metoprolol may elicit adverse effects, including bradycardia, hypotension, and bronchospasm, necessitating vigilant patient monitoring. Contraindications encompass conditions such as severe bradycardia, heart block, and decompensated heart failure, warranting cautious use of alternative therapies. An adept understanding of metoprolol's multifaceted clinical profile empowers healthcare professionals to navigate treatment decisions judiciously, promoting favorable patient outcomes in cardiovascular management.

agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
agent1_snippets.content: Excretion: Metoprolol and its metabolites are mainly excreted in the urine, accounting for about 95% of an oral dose. [25] Less than 5% to 10% of an oral dose is excreted unchanged in the urine, but this proportion can increase to 30% to 40% in poor CYP2D6 metabolizers. The renal clearance of metoprolol is about 1 L/h/kg. The elimination half-life of metoprolol ranges from 3 to 7 hours, depending on the dose, formulation, enantiomer, and metabolic phenotype.

agent1_snippets.title: Metoprolol -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
agent1_snippets.content: Pediatric: The safety and efficacy of metoprolol in pediatric patients have not been established. Limited data is available from clinical trials and case reports on the use of metoprolol in children for various indications, such as hypertension, arrhythmias, and heart failure. [30] The pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of metoprolol in children may differ from those in adults due to developmental factors such as body weight, organ maturation, enzyme activity, and drug interactions. Therefore, metoprolol should only be used in these patients under the guidance of a pediatric cardiologist. Patients in this group who use metoprolol should be monitored closely for blood pressure, heart rate, growth, development, and adverse effects.

agent1_snippets.content: Hypersensitivity: Metoprolol can cause hypersensitivity reactions in some patients allergic to metoprolol or related derivatives, such as atenolol or propranolol. The symptoms of hypersensitivity may include rash, itching, swelling, difficulty breathing, or anaphylaxis (a severe and potentially life-threatening reaction). agent1_snippets.title: 24-Hour Urine Collection and Analysis -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
agent1_snippets.content: For pheochromocytoma (metanephrine) urine testing, it is recommended that patients avoid food, caffeine, smoking, and strenuous exercise for 8 to 12 hours before sample collection. Many medications can interfere with metanephrine levels, leading to false positives. Drugs such as acetaminophen, tricyclic antidepressants (eg, amitriptyline), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (eg, buspirone), alpha-blockers (eg, tamsulosin), haloperidol, labetalol, levodopa, lamotrigine, aripiprazole, and possibly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors can elevate plasma and urine metanephrines. Notably, it is estimated that drug interference causes up to 20% of false-positive results, with tricyclic antidepressants and the alpha-blocker phenoxybenzamine accounting for up to 45% of these cases. Therefore, when possible, these medications should be discontinued 10 to 14 days before testing. [8] [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Selective Beta-1 Blockers -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The cardio-selective beta-blockers include atenolol, betaxolol, bisoprolol, esmolol, acebutolol, metoprolol, and nebivolol. [1] FDA-approved uses of beta-1-selective blockers include hypertension, chronic stable angina, heart failure, post-myocardial infarction, and decreased left ventricular function after a recent myocardial infarction. Non-FDA-approved (off-label) uses include migraine prophylaxis, treatment of arrhythmias, tremor reduction, and the symptomatic treatment of anxiety disorders. [2] Their use is associated with decreased morbidity and mortality for post-myocardial infarction. Treatment with beta-1 blockers reduces the risk of stroke, coronary artery disease, and congestive heart failure (for example, metoprolol succinate has proven mortality benefit in the treatment of heart failure, and it is the extended-release formulation of metoprolol).

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1170
agent2_snippets.content: Beta‑blocking agents are a pharmacologic class that antagonize beta‑adrenergic receptors, modulating cardiovascular and sympathetic functions. The class comprises atenolol, bisoprolol fumarate, bucindolol, bupranolol, dihydroalprenolol, indenolol, jodokyanopindolol, levobetaxolol hydrochloride, metoprolol, nadolol, nipradilol, oxprenolol‑hydrochlorid, penbutolol, prizidilol, propranolol, spirendolol, and 5‑hydroxypropafenone; 5‑hydroxypropafenone is synonymous with propafenon and is also classified as a beta‑blocking agent. Many of these agents—specifically propranolol, bucindolol, dipyridamol, indenolol, nipradilol, prizidilol and oxprenolol‑hydrochlorid—are additionally assigned to the vasodilator (vazodilatancia) class, as are the “námelové alkaloidy”. Dihydroergokryptin shows an inverse relationship to vasodilatancia, indicating antagonism of vasodilatory activity. Drug‑interaction constraints include a contraindication between metoprolol and propafenon and a separate contraindication between propafenon and propranolol, prohibiting their co‑administration. Recognizing the shared beta‑adrenergic receptor target, the overlapping vasodilatory properties, and the specific contraindicated combinations is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 799
agent2_snippets.content: 5‑hydroxypropafenone is mapped to propafenon, indicating that it is essentially the same compound or a closely related metabolite. Propafenon is contraindicated for use together with the beta‑blocker metoprolol, and it is also contraindicated with another beta‑blocker, propranolol. These contraindications suggest that co‑administration of propafenon with metoprolol or propranolol may lead to adverse drug interactions, likely due to overlapping effects on cardiac conduction and rhythm control. Clinicians should avoid prescribing propafenon concurrently with metoprolol or propranolol and should recognize that 5‑hydroxypropafenone should be treated as propafenon in therapeutic decision‑making.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1207
agent2_snippets.content: Beta‑blocking agents are a heterogeneous class of drugs that antagonize β‑adrenergic receptors, thereby diminishing sympathetic stimulation of the myocardium and peripheral vasculature. The class comprises bisoprolol fumarate, butoxamin, bucindolol, bupranolol, dihydroalprenolol, indenolol, jodokyanopindolol, karteolol, levobetaxolol, levobetaxolol hydrochloride, nadolol, nipradilol, oxprenolol, oxprenolol‑hydrochloride, penbutolol, pindolol, prizidilol, propranolol, spirendolol, dipyridamol, and combination products that pair a beta blocker with a diuretic. These agents are employed in the management of hypertension, angina, cardiac arrhythmias, and heart failure. Several beta‑blockers—specifically oxprenolol, oxprenolol‑hydrochloride, bucindolol, dipyridamol, indenolol, nipradilol, pindolol, prizidilol, propranolol, and spirendolol—exhibit additional vasodilatory activity, creating an overlap between β‑adrenergic antagonism and vascular smooth‑muscle relaxation. Nimodipine, a calcium‑channel blocker, also acts as a vasodilator, reinforcing its role in promoting vascular relaxation. In contrast, dihydroergokryptin lacks vasodilatory effect, representing an inverse relationship to vasodilation within this therapeutic landscape. The dual properties of β‑blockade and vasodilation in many of these drugs inform individualized therapeutic choices when simultaneous heart‑rate control and peripheral vasodilation are desired.

agent2_snippets.content: Beta blocking agents are a pharmacologic class that antagonize beta‑adrenergic receptors, particularly beta‑1 receptors, and are employed in the treatment of cardiovascular disorders and in ophthalmic glaucoma therapy. The class includes individual drugs such as 5‑hydroxypropafenone (propafenon), CGP 20712A, befunolol, betaxolol‑hydrochloride, bucindolol, bupranolol, cyanopindolol, dexefaroxan, dihydroalprenolol, ersentilide, indenolol, jodokyanopindolol, levobetaxolol, levobetaxolol hydrochloride, levobunolol, levobunolol hydrochloride, metoprolol, nadolol, nipradilol, oxprenolol‑hydrochloride, penbutolol, prizidilol, propranolol, spirendolol, and combination formulations such as beta blockers + diuretics. A child subcategory, “antagonisté beta‑1‑adrenergních receptorů,” denotes the beta‑1‑selective antagonists. Beta blocking agents are a subclass of sympatholytika and serve as the active ingredients in ophthalmic antiglaucoma beta‑adrenergic blocking agents; antiglaukomové látky, the Czech term for glaucoma medications, include levobunolol, befunolol and betaxolol‑hydrochloride, which lower intra‑ocular pressure by reducing aqueous humor production. The class is linked to anxiolytika, reflecting possible overlapping therapeutic uses, and may be formulated together with other compounds targeting beta‑adrenergic receptors (has_entry_combination relationship). Contraindicated interactions are noted between adrenergic beta‑antagonists and the miotic agent methacholin, as well as between metoprolol and propafenon and between propafenon and propranolol. The same mechanism of beta‑adrenergic antagonism underlies their use as ophthalmic antiglaucoma preparations, where they decrease intra‑ocular pressure. The class includes a broad spectrum of compounds such as CGP 20712A, befunolol, bisoprolol fumarate, betaxolol‑hydrochloride, bupranolol, cyanopindolol, dexefaroxan, dihydroalprenolol, ersentilide, jodokyanopindolol, levobetaxolol, levobetaxolol hydrochloride, levobunolol, levobunolol hydrochloride, metipranolol, nadolol, penbutolol, spirendolol, timolol, betaxolol and levobunolol. These agents serve as the active ingredients in the subgroup “beta‑blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics,” which includes timolol, betaxolol and metipranolol, highlighting their dual systemic and topical ocular roles. In Czech terminology, the class “antiglaukomové látky” (antiglaucoma agents) comprises levobunolol, befunolol, betaxolol‑hydrochloride, levobunolol hydrochloride and related beta‑adrenergic blockers, reinforcing their classification as ophthalmic antiglaucoma beta‑adrenergic antagonists. A notable safety consideration is the contraindication of timolol in patients with bronchospasm, reflecting the risk of bronchoconstriction associated with non‑selective beta blockade. Recognizing these interconnections and contraindications supports optimized patient care across cardiovascular, ocular, and neuropsychiatric domains.
